title: Rock Core Orientation Technology Using Resistivity Method
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"kxz9n","text":"Restoring the original orientation of core samples in the formation is crucial for geological engineers to better understand the physical properties of subsurface rocks, holding significant importance for engineering operations such as hydraulic fracturing design. However, over 99% of cores worldwide are acquired via conventional drilling coring, making it difficult to determine their orientation during the trip out of the hole. While directional coring and cutting core into cubes for paleomagnetic testing aim to achieve core orientation, the complexity of these processes prevents their widespread use on most cores. To address this, this paper develops an electrical resistivity-based core orientation technique suitable for the more universally available cylindrical cores. It obtains resistivity scanning images of the core, which are then meticulously correlated with downhole electrical imaging logging results. This enables core orientation, providing exploration professionals with the means to gain geological insights incorporating directional information.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"ifd1e","text":"Materials and Methods (procedural / methodology content present on these pages):","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"10xqj","text":"- To achieve in-situ parameter measurements on core samples, first determine the fluid saturation corresponding to the specific well depth. For high-maturity gas reservoirs, the water saturation at the target depth can be directly referenced. A full-diameter core sample is then fully saturated with formation water. A gas-displacing-water process is employed to establish saturation conditions consistent with those in the formation. Simultaneously, the cylindrical sample radial resistivity measurement method is used to test the resistivity values in different directions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rjaec","text":"- The radial resistivity formula for rock core is given as follows (equation as shown on page):","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jqvit","text":"  ρ = (K / K') L R    (Equation 1)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fnm6y","text":"  where ρ denotes resistivity, in Ω·m;","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"udcxi","text":"  K(α) is defined by an integral expression shown on the page (K' denotes the derivative of K);","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bjuzn","text":"  2α denotes the supplementary angle to the central angle of the curved electrode sheet contacting the core;","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jxn1d","text":"  L denotes the core length, in m;","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"41dsi","text":"  R denotes the apparent resistance value, in Ω.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rovuw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"128dn","text":"Taking the 159 sample of ST12 as an example, the workflow of core orientation using the resistivity method is described as follows:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"nmyqh","text":"Preparatory notes found on these pages:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nlpy7","text":"- Determine fluid saturation corresponding to the specific well depth before measurements.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"p7lfr","text":"- For high-maturity gas reservoirs, reference the water saturation at the target depth.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"s6jla","text":"- Ensure core saturation conditions replicate formation conditions (saturate with formation water and use gas-displacing-water process to reach desired saturation).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"rmkax","text":"- Prepare to perform radial resistivity measurements and to divide/mark the core circumference into N equal segments with a reference point \"O\".","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"nlj4b","text":"Items and materials explicitly mentioned on these pages:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dupoh","text":"- Full-diameter cylindrical core samples","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4y7gc","text":"- Formation water","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3nk4q","text":"- Gas (for gas-displacing water process)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"knzuy","text":"- Equipment/method for radial resistivity measurement of cylindrical samples (radial resistivity measurement method)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"okkrr","text":"- Downhole electrical imaging logging (used for correlation with core resistivity scans)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"sytzq","text":"- Curved electrode sheet contacting the core (part of the radial resistivity measurement apparatus)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tcm4x","text":"- Apparent resistance measurement capability/instrument to obtain R (apparent resistance, in Ω)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kudkz","text":"- Means to mark/divide the core circumference into N equal segments and a reference point \"O\" (for segment-based measurements)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ihsxr","text":"- Tools to measure core length (L, in m)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1pmmm","text":"- Electrical imaging logging tool and associated individual electrodes (raw data acquisition; ~1,000+ data points per meter)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"s9jkp","text":"- Data processing/software to generate FMI-DYN (or equivalent) plots and to convert resistivity values to conductivity values for imaging interpretation","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: After fully saturating the full-diameter sample with water, different water saturation states of the full-diameter core are achieved through gas displacing water. Using the radial resistivity measurement method (with the curved electrode pieces of the testing device set at a 90° angle; for the testing principle, refer to He JH, Li M, Zhou KM, Zeng L, Li N, Yang Y, Xiao D, Huang M. Radial resistivity measurement method for cylindrical core samples. Interpretation. 2020 Nov;8(4), T1071–T1080. https://doi.org/10.1190/INT-2019-0213.1), the resistivity values in various directions are measured under different water saturation conditions. In this example, the resistivity is measured every 30°. The core is divided into 12 equal segments at 30° intervals, labeled P 1 , P 2 , ..., P 12 . The measured resistivity values are provided in S2.

Based on the calculated resistivity values, the conductivity values in each direction are computed using the reciprocal relationship between conductivity and resistivity. Specific calculated values can be found in Table S2 in the attachment.

According to the water saturation data from the corresponding well depth provided by the field, the conductivity values converted from the resistivity measured under the closest water saturation condition in the radial resistivity test are selected and compared with the conductivity values obtained from the electrical imaging logging.

Based on the radial resistivity measurement results, it is straightforward to identify the rotation angles θ max and θ min corresponding to the maximum resistivity value R max and the minimum resistivity value R min respectively, and mark these orientations on the core. It is noted that among the conductivity values measured every 30°, the resistivity values at 90° and 240° are the maximum (561.43 Ω·m) and minimum (437.23 Ω·m), respectively. We denote 90° as the angle θ max with the maximum resistivity, and 240° as the angle θ min with the minimum resistivity. In the core test results, the point with the minimum conductivity is P 4 , and the point with the maximum conductivity is P 9 .

A comparison is made with the conductivity values from the electrical imaging log. At the corresponding depth of the ST12 well in the electrical imaging log, the brightest and darkest values are identified, which are represented using conductivity values.Record the original azimuth (orientation in the formation) of these points. The Hue, Saturation, and Brightness (HSB values) of the color at each point are read and recorded as shown in Table 1. （Using Windows' built-in "Paint" tool, you can achieve this operation. First, use the "Color Picker" to select the area you want to test, then click on "Edit Colors" to view the hue, saturation, and brightness values.） %s %s On the corresponding electrical imaging log, the darkest point is observed at 212°, representing the location with the lowest resistivity. The electrical imaging log is then divided into 12 equal segments, and the corresponding values are read from each segment. It can be observed that among these 12 points, the position with the darkest color and the lowest resistivity is at 212°, with a brightness value of 27, while the brightest position with the highest resistivity is at 62°, with a brightness value of 179.

Since the colors in the electrical imaging log reflect the relative magnitude of conductivity, identifying the darkest and brightest points at the corresponding depth is particularly important. Based on the above, the measurement points can be labeled as Q 1 , Q 2 , Q 3 , ..., Q 12 at 2°, 32°, 62°, etc., respectively. The position with the darkest color and the lowest resistivity is at 212°, recorded as Q 8 , while the brightest position with the highest resistivity is at 62°, with a brightness value of 179, recorded as Q3.
By comparing the relative magnitudes of radial conductivity and electrical imaging logging, it can be observed that P 9 corresponds to the same location as Q 8 , and P 4 corresponds to Q 3 .

P 1 represents the starting point at 0° on the core. By comparing the position of P 1 in the electrical imaging log image, it can be determined that the 28° angle on the core aligns with true north direction in the formation.
